network.overall_strategy: The network prioritizes literature-supported prebiotic aspartate sources by combining (i) hydrothermal CO2 fixation to central keto-acid hubs, especially pyruvate and oxaloacetate, (ii) surface cyanosulfidic and HCN chemistry that generates HCN-derived branch intermediates and direct aspartate-producing oligomer routes, and (iii) biochemical/prebiotic conversion of oxaloacetate through Bucherer-Bergs and transamination. Ten distinct pathways to aspartic acid are represented by alternative sequences converging on four hub molecules: HCN, pyruvate, oxaloacetate, and fumarate. The design explicitly incorporates the major productive route (HCN oligomer hydrolysis), the recently demonstrated Bucherer-Bergs route from oxaloacetate, Cu2+/pyridoxamine transamination of oxaloacetate, cyanoacetylene chemistry, fumarate ammoniation, and direct hydrothermal Fe0-mediated synthesis. Environmental interconnection is shown by hydrothermal generation of pyruvate and oxaloacetate precursors, surface concentration/photochemistry of HCN and cyanoacetylene, and biochemical assembly steps that convert shared intermediates into the target.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formaldehyde/glycolaldehyde feedstock

conditions.other: alkaline vent mixing; H2-rich serpentinization conditions

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed CO2 hydrogenation and Fischer-Tropsch-type C-C coupling generate reduced C1 and C2 carbonyl compounds that feed surface and protometabolic chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe-bearing minerals and vent conditions generate reduced carbonyl feedstocks from CO2 and H2, providing plausible precursors to downstream pyruvate and HCN-linked chemistry in alkaline vent settings (Preiner et al., 2020; Kitadai, 2015; Santos-Carballal et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fe0-mediated pyruvate formation from CO2

conditions.other: anoxic CO2-rich system; hydroxylamine-compatible network context

reactions.mechanism: Reduced iron drives CO2 fixation to central carbonyl/carboxylate products including pyruvate under mild hydrothermal conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Fe0-mediated CO2 reduction produces pyruvate under prebiotically plausible hydrothermal conditions, establishing pyruvate as a central hub for oxaloacetate and amino acid synthesis (Varma et al., 2018; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal generation of glyoxylate from reduced C2 carbonyl chemistry
reactions.mechanism: Iron-sulfur-mediated CO2 fixation and carbonyl rearrangement furnish glyoxylate as a key C2 protometabolic node.
reactions.reasoning: Glyoxylate is a central feedstock in non-enzymatic protometabolic TCA-like chemistry and links hydrothermal carbon fixation to oxaloacetate formation; its role in pyruvate-glyoxylate chemistry is emphasized in protometabolic studies (Muchowska et al., 2019; Eschenmoser & Krishnamurthy, 2017).

reactions.name: Protometabolic aldol route to oxaloacetate from pyruvate and glyoxylate

conditions.other: CO2-rich anoxic protometabolic network; oxaloacetate treated as transient due to rapid decarboxylation

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate and glyoxylate enter non-enzymatic protometabolic carbon-addition chemistry to access oxaloacetate or oxaloacetate-equivalent keto diacids.
reactions.reasoning: Oxaloacetate is the key nexus for aspartate synthesis, and pyruvate-glyoxylate chemistry is among the principal literature-supported routes to OAA-related intermediates despite instability and rapid decarboxylation (Eschenmoser & Krishnamurthy, 2017; Muchowska et al., 2019; Guzman & Martin, 2008).

reactions.name: Surface HCN formation by UV/spark photochemistry

conditions.other: strong UV irradiation; evaporitic pools and volcanic plume chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical nitrile generation from reduced carbon and nitrogen feedstocks supplies concentrated HCN for oligomerization and cyanoacetylene chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: HCN is the dominant hub for productive abiotic aspartate synthesis via oligomerization and related chemistry; surface photochemical environments are necessary to concentrate and process cyanide precursors under early Earth UV flux (Matthews & Moser, 1966; Ferris et al., 1978; Sagan & Khare, 1971).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN and higher HCN polymers

conditions.other: requires [HCN] > 10^-2 M; optimal around pH 9.2; eutectic freezing can concentrate HCN to ~10 M

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated cyanide oligomerizes through dimer/trimer/tetramer stages to DAMN and higher polymers that serve as direct amino-acid-yielding reservoirs upon hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization is the most productive known route to prebiotic aspartate, with DAMN as a key branch intermediate; oligomerization is favored at pH ~9.2 and by freezing concentration (Ferris, Donner & Lobo, 1974; Ferris et al., 1978; Miyakawa, Cleaves & Miller, 2002).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomers to aspartic acid

conditions.other: acid hydrolysis analogous to 6 N HCl, 110C, 24 h analytical release of amino acids from HCN oligomers

reactions.mechanism: Acidic hydrolysis of HCN oligomer backbones releases amino acids, with aspartate among the major products.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomers reproducibly yields aspartic acid as a major product rather than a trace component, making this the strongest single source route in the literature (Ferris, Donner & Lobo, 1974; Ferris et al., 1978; Matthews & Moser, 1966).

reactions.name: Surface cyanoacetylene formation from cyanide-rich photochemistry

conditions.other: electric discharge/photochemical atmosphere-to-surface transfer; weakly reducing localized settings

reactions.mechanism: High-energy photochemistry converts cyanide-rich feedstocks into cyanoacetylene, a reactive C3 nitrile branch point.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanoacetylene is a documented atmospheric discharge product and a key branch intermediate leading to both aspartate and cytosine in aqueous NH3/HCN chemistry (Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Miller, 1953; Shapiro, 1999).

reactions.name: Cyanoacetylene + HCN + NH3 route to aspartic acid
reactions.mechanism: C3 cyanoacetylene couples with HCN and ammonia in aqueous alkaline solution to generate aspartate through nitrile addition and hydrolysis chemistry distinct from classical Strecker synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: This direct cyanoacetylene pathway is specifically reported to generate aspartic acid, asparagine, and cytosine at about pH 9 and room temperature, providing a cyanide-rich surface route to the target (Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel, 1966).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of oxaloacetate to N-carbamoyl aspartate and aspartate

conditions.other: aqueous NaCN + NH4HCO3 equivalent conditions; neutral to slightly basic pH

reactions.mechanism: Oxaloacetate undergoes Bucherer-Bergs reactivity with cyanide and ammonium bicarbonate to form N-carbamoyl aspartate, which hydrolyzes to aspartate while also enabling dihydroorotate formation.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs pathway from oxaloacetate is a recently demonstrated and highly relevant prebiotic route to aspartate, directly linking amino acid and pyrimidine precursor synthesis through N-carbamoyl aspartate and dihydroorotate (Pulletikurti, Yadav, Springsteen & Krishnamurthy, 2022).

reactions.name: Cu2+-pyridoxamine transamination of oxaloacetate to aspartate

conditions.other: 5-10 mM reactants; 50 mM NH4HCO3 buffer; pH 6-10; ~1 h reaction window before substantial OAA loss

reactions.mechanism: Metal-assisted PLP-analog transamination transfers amino functionality from pyridoxamine to oxaloacetate faster than decarboxylation during the initial reaction window.
reactions.reasoning: Cu2+-catalyzed transamination of oxaloacetate by pyridoxamine is the best-characterized biologically parallel prebiotic route, reaching ~5% aspartate in 1 h but limited by competing OAA decarboxylation (Harrison et al., 2023; Mayer et al., 2021; Dherbassy et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Oxaloacetate decarboxylation to pyruvate

conditions.other: half-life ~1-2 h at 25C near neutral pH; accelerated by divalent metals

reactions.mechanism: Spontaneous beta-keto acid decarboxylation converts oxaloacetate to pyruvate, competing with all OAA-to-aspartate routes.
reactions.reasoning: Rapid oxaloacetate decarboxylation is the dominant bottleneck in prebiotic aspartate synthesis and must be explicitly represented because it diverts OAA toward pyruvate and alanine-forming chemistry (Guzman & Martin, 2008; Barge et al., 2019; Harrison et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Photochemical OAA-to-fumarate branch via malate/fumarate chemistry

conditions.other: photocatalytic reduction/dehydration network inspired by ZnS malate/fumarate chemistry; represented here with allowed surface minerals as concentration/photochemical supports

reactions.mechanism: Oxaloacetate enters a photochemical redox/dehydration branch that accesses fumarate, a known rTCA-related node and precursor to aspartate by ammoniation.
reactions.reasoning: Fumarate is a documented prebiotic branch point connected to oxaloacetate/malate chemistry in non-enzymatic rTCA analogs and photocatalytic networks, and it is specifically identified in the literature as a node leading to aspartate via ammonia addition; this connection is somewhat schematic here because the cited studies emphasize ZnS rather than the allowed output mineral list (Guzman & Martin, 2008; Zhou & Guzman, 2016; Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2020).

reactions.name: Michael addition of ammonia to fumarate forming aspartic acid

conditions.other: alkaline conditions with high NH3/NH4+ concentration; low non-enzymatic yield

reactions.mechanism: Non-enzymatic conjugate addition of ammonia to fumarate affords aspartate, analogous to the reverse biological aspartase reaction.
reactions.reasoning: Although low yielding without enzymatic catalysis, ammonia addition to fumarate is thermodynamically feasible under alkaline, ammonia-rich conditions and is a literature-recognized distinct route to aspartate (Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2020; Harrison et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fe0/hydroxylamine direct route to aspartate

conditions.other: anoxic CO2 reduction network with hydroxylamine as nitrogen source

reactions.mechanism: Direct Fe0-driven CO2 reduction in the presence of a reactive nitrogen donor furnishes amino acid products including aspartate within a broader protometabolic network.
reactions.reasoning: Aspartate has been detected in Fe0-mediated CO2 reduction experiments when hydroxylamine serves as nitrogen source, making this a direct hydrothermal source route distinct from OAA transamination or HCN oligomer hydrolysis (Varma et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Surface UV conversion of HCN and water to formamide

conditions.other: strong UV irradiation in shallow pools

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical hydration/oxidation of cyanide-rich surface inventories generates formamide, a versatile prebiotic solvent/reactant linked to nitrile chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is a well-established product/intermediate in UV-driven cyanide chemistry and helps connect cyanide-rich surface environments to broader prebiotic synthesis space; included here as a network-supported branch from the HCN hub (Sagan & Khare, 1971; Muñoz Caro et al., 2002).

pathways.name: Direct HCN oligomer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: HCN generated in surface environments oligomerizes to DAMN/higher polymers and hydrolyzes to release aspartate as a major product.

pathways.name: Frozen-concentrated HCN oligomer route
pathways.description: The same HCN oligomer route operates under eutectic freezing concentration, improving cyanide concentration before hydrolysis to aspartate.

pathways.name: Cyanoacetylene coupling route
pathways.description: Surface HCN is converted to cyanoacetylene, which reacts with HCN and NH3 in alkaline aqueous solution to form aspartate.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal OAA to Bucherer-Bergs route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation generates pyruvate and glyoxylate, which feed oxaloacetate formation; oxaloacetate is trapped by cyanide/ammonium chemistry to form aspartate.

pathways.name: Transamination route from oxaloacetate
pathways.description: Oxaloacetate formed through protometabolic hydrothermal chemistry is rapidly aminated by Cu2+/pyridoxamine before decarboxylation dominates.

pathways.name: Fumarate ammoniation route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal oxaloacetate precursors feed a surface photochemical branch to fumarate, followed by non-enzymatic ammonia addition to aspartate.

pathways.name: Direct Fe0-hydroxylamine hydrothermal route
pathways.description: Aspartate arises directly in an Fe0-mediated CO2 reduction network with hydroxylamine as nitrogen donor under mild hydrothermal conditions.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface HCN then Bucherer-Bergs hybrid route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal oxaloacetate formation converges with surface-generated HCN; the two streams meet in Bucherer-Bergs conversion to aspartate.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal carbonyl feedstock to HCN oligomer route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced carbonyl compounds seed surface HCN production, which then proceeds through oligomerization and hydrolysis to aspartate.

pathways.name: Pyruvate-regeneration constrained OAA route
pathways.description: Pyruvate formed hydrothermally regenerates transient oxaloacetate via glyoxylate chemistry; aspartate forms by transamination in competition with OAA decarboxylation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the strongest pathways in the network and aligns well with literature prioritizing HCN oligomer hydrolysis as a major aspartate source. HCN oligomerization to DAMN/higher polymers is well supported, and hydrolysis of oligomers yielding aspartate is strongly literature-backed. The main weakness is rxn_005: representing HCN formation from formaldehyde + NH3 is not a canonical prebiotic route and is oversimplified relative to spark/discharge or atmospheric photochemistry.
suggestions: Replace or refine rxn_005 to a more literature-supported atmospheric discharge or photochemical HCN source
Specify oligomer hydrolysis conditions more explicitly, such as acid hydrolysis or prolonged aqueous processing

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The frozen-concentrated HCN route is literature-supported in principle because eutectic freezing enhances HCN oligomerization and subsequent hydrolysis can yield aspartate. However, the pathway as presented uses the same reaction set as P1 and does not encode freezing-specific conditions, so its distinctiveness is underrepresented. Still, the core chemistry is plausible.
suggestions: Add explicit eutectic freezing conditions to distinguish this pathway from the standard aqueous oligomer route
Clarify whether DAMN is the dominant isolated precursor or part of a broader HCN polymer mixture

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The cyanoacetylene route is explicitly supported by Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel and is a valid alternative route to aspartate. The main concern is that rxn_008 collapses cyanoacetylene generation into a single HCN-to-cyanoacetylene conversion without representing the actual discharge chemistry, so precursor realism is limited. The final coupling step is nevertheless literature-aligned.
suggestions: Represent cyanoacetylene generation as discharge-derived atmospheric chemistry rather than direct conversion from HCN
Include pH ~9 aqueous conditions for the coupling step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway captures the important Bucherer-Bergs route from oxaloacetate and is among the better biologically relevant designs. rxn_010 is strongly aligned with the 2022 literature and correctly includes N-carbamoyl aspartate and dihydroorotate branching. The weaker steps are hydrothermal glyoxylate/OAA generation: rxn_003 and rxn_004 are plausible protometabolic proposals but are not strongly established under the exact stated conditions, and OAA instability remains a major unaddressed bottleneck.
suggestions: Explicitly address oxaloacetate instability and rapid downstream capture
Clarify whether rxn_004 proceeds via 4-hydroxy-2-ketoglutarate or related intermediates instead of direct one-step OAA formation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The final transamination step is literature-supported and biologically relevant, especially with Cu2+/pyridoxamine. However, the pathway is penalized by two major issues: uncertain OAA generation chemistry and the fact that pyridoxamine origin is not addressed, which the literature identifies as a major gap. In addition, Cu2+ catalysis competes with OAA decarboxylation, so sustained efficiency is limited.
suggestions: Include Cu2+ explicitly in rxn_011 conditions and note short reaction times
Acknowledge pyridoxamine as a poorly justified prebiotic input
Add explicit OAA regeneration or rapid capture logic

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the weakest major pathway. While fumarate is a legitimate network node in prebiotic metabolism, the presented conversion of OAA directly to fumarate in rxn_013 is underspecified and not faithful to known photochemical sequences, which usually involve malate or ZnS-mediated steps. rxn_014, non-enzymatic ammonia addition to fumarate, is thermodynamically possible but generally gives very low yields without enzymatic catalysis, so target achievement is poor.
suggestions: Insert malate explicitly as an intermediate if using photochemical fumarate generation
Use ZnS-based photochemistry or literature-supported rTCA analog chemistry instead of direct OAA-to-fumarate conversion
Treat fumarate ammoniation as a minor side route rather than a strong production pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This direct Fe0/hydroxylamine hydrothermal route is well aligned with the cited literature and is one of the best single-step target-achieving reactions in the network. Its limitation is network-level rather than reaction-level: it bypasses the main hub structure, so it contributes less to integrated topology than the OAA- and HCN-based routes.
suggestions: Clarify pressure and temperature ranges to match Fe0-mediated CO2 reduction studies more precisely
Connect this route more explicitly to shared carbon fixation intermediates for stronger network integration

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This hybrid route does show the intended hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical flow, which is a strong design feature. However, rxn_001 is chemically weak as written because CO2 + H2 directly yielding both formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde in one step is not a well-defined hydrothermal transformation. The rest of the pathway benefits from the strong Bucherer-Bergs end step, but upstream feedstock generation is too compressed.
suggestions: Split rxn_001 into more defensible carbon fixation steps such as formate/formaldehyde formation followed by C-C coupling
Better justify how HCN derived at the surface contributes to the Bucherer-Bergs chemistry on returning to pool conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway effectively links hydrothermal carbonyl generation to surface HCN chemistry and then to hydrolysis, but it relies on two oversimplified feedstock steps. The downstream HCN oligomer section is strong; the upstream hydrothermal-to-HCN conversion logic is much less literature-grounded. As a result, it is an attractive systems narrative but not among the most chemically secure pathways.
suggestions: Use atmospheric or photochemical HCN generation independently of formaldehyde feedstock unless a specific mechanistic link is justified
Retain the oligomer-hydrolysis core as the primary validated component

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway attempts to address the OAA instability problem through pyruvate regeneration, which is conceptually responsive to the literature. However, it remains chemically underdefined as a cyclic route and does not truly demonstrate sustained OAA regeneration under prebiotic conditions. The cycle-like logic is interesting but remains speculative, especially since the regeneration chemistry is not experimentally established as a self-sustaining network.
suggestions: Explicitly model the regeneration step sequence and associated oxidants/reductants
Avoid presenting this as a robust cycle unless turnover has been demonstrated
Emphasize it as a constrained speculative route rather than a primary source

evaluation.network_feedback:
The network is topologically strong and clearly designed around literature-relevant aspartate chemistry. It contains sufficient node counts, multiple hubs, real convergence on HCN, pyruvate, oxaloacetate, and fumarate, and good cross-environment connectivity. The strongest features are inclusion of the major literature-supported HCN oligomer hydrolysis pathway, the Bucherer-Bergs oxaloacetate route with N-carbamoyl aspartate/dihydroorotate branching, Cu2+/pyridoxamine transamination, and the Fe0/hydroxylamine hydrothermal route. These align well with the provided research findings.

The main weaknesses are chemical compression and occasional overstatement of uncertain steps. Several upstream hydrothermal reactions are too lumped or insufficiently supported in the exact forms written, especially direct CO2-to-formaldehyde/glycolaldehyde feedstock production and the one-step glyoxylate/OAA generation sequence. The OAA instability problem, which is the major literature bottleneck, is recognized implicitly by inclusion of decarboxylation but not fully handled mechanistically. The fumarate branch is especially weak. There is also a significant literature-gap issue around pyridoxamine availability, which the network uses as an input without justification.

Environmental interconnection is good overall, but the evaluation is limited by missing mineral-agent detail in the summarized network presentation. Because the system instructions require mineral agents for all hydrothermal and surface reactions, this omission would be a formal compliance problem unless the original full JSON included them. Literature alignment is good but not perfect: the network captures the right major routes, yet some reactions would need tighter condition fidelity and explicit citations to fully satisfy the specification.

Overall, this is a good but not approval-grade network. It is coherent, connected, and largely literature-informed, but it falls short of >=0.9 confidence because several critical steps are oversimplified and some pathway claims exceed current experimental support.
